core_meaning: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる means to show something to someone — you are the one actively displaying, presenting, or revealing something to another person. Unlike <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (something is visible to you) or <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る (you look at something), <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる puts you in the driver's seat: you decide to let someone else see it.

sentences.jp: <ruby>新<rt>あたら</rt></ruby>しいスマホ、ちょっと<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ。
sentences.translation: Hey, show me your new phone for a sec.
sentences.notes: The て-form request <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてよ is the most natural casual way to ask someone to show you something; <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてください becomes standard/polite.

sentences.jp: パスポートを<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せていただけますか。
sentences.translation: Could I ask you to show me your passport?
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せていただけますか is the polished request form used in service, official, or business contexts.

sentences.jp: <ruby>子<rt>こ</rt></ruby>どもたちに<ruby>手品<rt>てじな</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてあげたら、めちゃくちゃ<ruby>喜<rt>よろこ</rt></ruby>んだ。
sentences.translation: When I showed the kids a magic trick, they were absolutely delighted.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてあげる captures the nuance of doing something for someone's benefit; the auxiliary あげる signals the direction of the favor toward the kids.

sentences.jp: このプレゼンで、<ruby>私<rt>わたし</rt></ruby>たちの<ruby>実力<rt>じつりょく</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せよう。
sentences.translation: Let's show our true ability in this presentation.
sentences.notes: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is often used figuratively to mean 'demonstrate' or 'prove' a quality or capability, not just physically display an object.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>痛<rt>いた</rt></ruby>みを<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>に<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せないタイプだ。
sentences.translation: She's the type who doesn't show her pain to others.
sentences.notes: The negative <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せない highlights how <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる extends to revealing emotions or inner states, a very common and natural figurative use.

dont_use: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる should not be used when you simply want to say something is visible or appears a certain way — for that, use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える (e.g. <ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える, 'the mountain can be seen'). Also, don't confuse it with <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る when describing your own act of watching or looking — <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>る, not <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる. Finally, avoid over-using <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてあげる in contexts where it could sound condescending toward adults.

confused_with.contrast: A common mistake is saying 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる」when you mean the mountain can be seen — this sounds wrong because <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる requires an agent actively showing something to someone. <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える is the right word when something is naturally visible or comes into view without a deliberate action: 「<ruby>山<rt>やま</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える」. Use <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる when a person deliberately shows something to another, and <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える when something is passively perceptible.

confusion_set.use_when: You (an agent) deliberately show or display something to someone else.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>写真<rt>しゃしん</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せてくれた。— She showed me the photos.

confusion_set.use_when: You yourself look at or watch something (no second person receiving the show).
confusion_set.example: <ruby>映画<rt>えいが</rt></ruby>を<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>た。— I watched a movie.

confusion_set.use_when: Something is naturally visible or appears a certain way — no deliberate showing involved.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>窓<rt>まど</rt></ruby>から<ruby>海<rt>うみ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える。— You can see the ocean from the window.

frequency: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる is extremely common in everyday spoken and written Japanese across all registers. The request form <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せて(ください) is among the most frequently heard expressions in shops, offices, and casual conversation alike.
anki_hint: <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>せる = you are the one holding something out for another person's eyes — if no one is actively showing, reach for <ruby>見<rt>み</rt></ruby>える instead.
